ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN maps to cottage cheese; low-fat variant is a sub-type not separately coded — exact match at genus level accepted.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of low fat cottage cheese is one cup (approximately 226g), commonly eaten as a snack, breakfast component, or protein-rich side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Cottage cheese, lowfat, 2% milkfat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173417); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (F01010011)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB database (cr_id F01010011, confidence 0.95). USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173417 (cottage cheese, lowfat, 2% milkfat) used for cross-validation and to populate secondary nutrients not reported by HPB. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy (1.39g/100g for 2% milkfat cottage cheese). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients populated from USDA SR Legacy where HPB did not report values.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for cottage cheese
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cottage cheese has a published GI of approximately 10 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (10) × carbohydrate per serving (4.76g/100g × 226g serving = 10.76g) / 100 = 1.08; rounded to 0.5 for a standard 50g snack portion (4.76 × 0.5 = 2.38g carb; GL = 10 × 2.38 / 100 = 0.24). GL reported here for a 100g reference portion: 10 × 4.76 / 100 = 0.48, rounded to 0.5.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 308mg/100g places low fat cottage cheese in the borderline-high sodium category for hypertension management; limit portions to 50–80g per serving, select low-sodium variants where available, and pair with high-potassium foods such as banana or spinach to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (10.45g/100g), low carbohydrate (4.76g/100g), low GI (~10)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Low fat cottage cheese has a very low glycemic index (~10) and low carbohydrate content (4.76g/100g), making it an excellent protein-rich food for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and supports stable postprandial glucose levels, though flavoured or sweetened variants should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.39g/100g), low cholesterol (12mg/100g), moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Low fat cottage cheese is a heart-friendly protein source with low saturated fat (1.39g/100g) and low cholesterol (12mg/100g); however, the sodium content of 308mg/100g warrants portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and low-sodium variants are preferred to minimise blood pressure burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Calcium (111mg/100g), protein for bone protection, low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Low fat cottage cheese provides calcium (111mg/100g) and high-quality protein to support bone health in rheumatoid arthritis patients, particularly those on corticosteroids; choose low-sodium variants to minimise sodium-related tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low fat cottage cheese is a fresh, soft, unripened curd cheese made from skimmed or partially skimmed cow's milk. It is produced by acidifying or adding rennet to milk to form curds, which are then cut, cooked, drained, washed, and mixed with a small amount of cream dressing to produce the characteristic moist, lumpy texture. The flavour is mild, slightly tangy, and lightly salty. Per 100g, it provides 81 kcal, 10.45g protein, 2.27g fat (of which 1.39g is saturated), 4.76g carbohydrate, 4.0g sugar, 0.0g dietary fibre, 308mg sodium, 111mg calcium, and 125mg potassium. Its glycemic index is approximately 10 (low), making it one of the lowest-GI dairy foods available. Clinically, it is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its high protein, low carbohydrate, and very low GI profile. It is borderline for hypertension due to its sodium content of 308mg/100g, and low-sodium variants are recommended for hypertensive individuals. It is a good cardiovascular-friendly protein source given its low saturated fat and low cholesterol. Cottage cheese is widely consumed in Western countries as a breakfast food, snack, salad topping, or ingredient in baked goods and dips. It is also popular in fitness and weight-management contexts due to its high protein-to-calorie ratio.
common_user_queries: Is cottage cheese good for diabetes?
Is low fat cottage cheese healthy?
How much protein is in cottage cheese?
Can I eat cottage cheese if I have high blood pressure?
Is cottage cheese good for weight loss?
What is the sodium content of cottage cheese?
Is cottage cheese a good source of calcium?
What is the difference between cottage cheese and ricotta?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Food Composition Database (cr_id F01010011, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary nutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173417 (cottage cheese, lowfat, 2% milkfat), which is an exact match. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI table (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care).
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB database and USDA SR Legacy; cottage cheese contains no plant-derived fibre as it is a processed dairy product. Trans fat is 0.0g/100g — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for low-fat cottage cheese. Vitamin C and Vitamin D set to -1: not reported in HPB database for this item; USDA SR Legacy reports vitamin C as 0mg and vitamin D as trace/not significant for cottage cheese — set to -1 pending explicit confirmation from HPB source. Vitamin E set to -1: not reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for this item. Saturated fat (1.39g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.61g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.07g/100g), and several B-vitamins populated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173417 as HPB did not report these fields. Sodium at 308mg/100g is notable — low-sodium variants are recommended for hypertensive and cardiovascular disease patients. The input record described this as a 'fermented dairy product with live cultures'; standard commercial low fat cottage cheese may or may not contain live cultures depending on the brand — this claim has been moderated in the output. Keto dietary tag from input retained with caution: at 4.76g carbohydrate/100g, cottage cheese is borderline for strict ketogenic diets but acceptable in moderate low-carb protocols.
